title: AML Agents Briefing — Tuesday, 16 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Monday, the sixteenth of June 2026. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across the Asia-Pacific region today. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got APP fraud figures that will unsettle anyone running a payments compliance programme, a significant AUSTRAC enforcement action to unpack, and two deadlines that should be on every MLRO's calendar right now.

ALEX: Let's start with the fraud numbers. UK Finance has published its 2025 authorised push payment fraud data, and the headline is not a good one. Authorised push payment, or APP, fraud reached one point two eight billion pounds in 2025, a four per cent increase on the prior year.

JORDAN: Which matters here because?

ALEX: Because the Payment Systems Regulator's mandatory reimbursement regime was supposed to bend that curve downward. It did briefly, but the recovery in fraud volumes tells us the criminal ecosystem adapted faster than the policy response. That dynamic is not unique to the United Kingdom. We're seeing the same pattern play out across APAC.

JORDAN: And Bank Negara Malaysia published a typology bulletin in April flagging almost exactly this, investment scams routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges, with mule-account indicators including rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes and inbound flows from multiple unrelated retail senders.

ALEX: So what's the action for listeners?

JORDAN: If you're an MLRO or head of financial crime at a digital bank or e-money institution, pull your post-onboarding velocity rules this week. The BNM typology is specific: accounts that spike in transaction volume within days of activation, receiving from multiple unrelated retail sources and immediately pushing outbound, that is your mule-account signature. Compare your current detection rules against those indicators and document the gap analysis before your next risk committee.

ALEX: Moving on to enforcement, and this one is directly relevant to anyone operating in Australia. AUSTRAC announced a civil penalty of forty-seven million Australian dollars against a major registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation.

JORDAN: The three failure modes AUSTRAC identified there are worth naming clearly. Transaction monitoring that wasn't calibrated to the exchange's actual customer base, mule-account detection that didn't catch the velocity patterns we were just discussing, and SMR submissions that were late. That last one is a separate and compounding liability.

ALEX: So for VASP compliance officers, what's the read-across?

JORDAN: Three things. First, your transaction monitoring scenarios need to be tuned to your own risk profile, not a generic ruleset you inherited at implementation. Second, your SMR governance pack should include a documented escalation timeline so you can demonstrate timeliness if AUSTRAC comes asking. Third, check your AUSTRAC annual compliance report obligation. The reporting period for calendar year 2024 is already past due, and late lodgement under the AML and CTF Act attracts daily civil penalties. If your report hasn't been lodged, that needs to be addressed today, not this week.

ALEX: On the crypto side more broadly, Bitcoin volatility tied to geopolitical tension is creating real compliance pressure for virtual asset service providers across the region. When prices move sharply on conflict-driven news, transaction volumes spike, sanctions exposure widens, and automated monitoring systems can lag.

JORDAN: For VASPs running know-your-transaction or KYT systems, this is the moment to confirm that your vendor integration covers both inbound and outbound flows in near real time, and that your sanctions screening refresh rate is adequate during high-volume periods. If you're relying on batch screening, a conflict-driven volume spike is exactly when that gap becomes a liability.

ALEX: Let's turn to obligations, because there are two deadlines that need attention. Jordan, you want to take these?

JORDAN: Yes. First, Indonesian regulated entities, under POJK 12 slash POJK dot 01 slash 2017, the independent annual review of your AML and CFT programme covering the calendar year 2024 period is due by the thirtieth of June. Findings go to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners. If your internal audit function hasn't commenced that review, you are cutting it very close.

ALEX: And for Hong Kong?

JORDAN: The HKMA AML and CFT Guideline section eleven annual self-assessment return for calendar year 2024 is due by the thirtieth of September. It requires Internal Audit independent validation and submission via the HKMA prescribed Excel template. September sounds distant, but if your Internal Audit team needs to validate the findings, the substantive work needs to start now. Put that on your July planning calendar this week.

ALEX: Before we close, what to watch. AUSTRAC has published detailed Tranche 2 industry guidance for legal practitioners, covering customer-engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing, and the legal professional privilege carve-outs that the sector has been waiting on. If you have legal practitioners in your customer base, or if you're advising a law firm on its new AML obligations, that guidance is required reading.

JORDAN: For listeners in the coming week, three concrete steps. Review your mule-account detection rules against the BNM typology indicators. Confirm your AUSTRAC annual compliance report is lodged if you're an Australian reporting entity. And for Indonesian entities, verify your calendar year 2024 AML programme review is underway before the thirtieth of June.

JORDAN: That's your briefing for the sixteenth of June. Full source material, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are at amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Worth bookmarking if you haven't already.

ALEX: We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
